channel.title: Particle-Physics on Archibald
channel.description: Recent content in Particle-Physics on Archibald

item.title: They Built Three Detectors To Find Dark Matter. They Found The Sun.
item.description:
Three separate experiments, on three continents, run by three different international collaborations, dug underground to find the same thing. LUX-ZEPLIN in South Dakota. XENONnT in Italy. PandaX-4T in Sichuan, China. The largest, most sensitive dark matter detectors ever built. Between them: hundreds of researchers, decades of work, hundreds of millions of dollars in liquid xenon buried under mountains to get away from cosmic noise.

All three found solar neutrinos.

Not dark matter - the sun. Boron-8 neutrinos streaming through the rock, through the shielding, through the xenon, leaving the faint traces the experiments were built to catch for something else. At nearly identical statistical significance: 2.73 sigma, 2.64 sigma, below the five-sigma discovery threshold, confirming each other’s solar neutrino detections.

item.title: Sixty-Five Billion Per Second. You Don't Notice. Neither Does the Earth.
item.description:
Sixty-five billion solar neutrinos pass through every square centimeter of your hand every second.

You don’t notice. The Sun produces them in the nuclear reactions that keep it burning, and they travel outward at nearly the speed of light, and eight minutes later they reach Earth, and they go straight through it. The rock. The water. The iron core. You. The planet is transparent to neutrinos the way a window is transparent to light, except less so — a neutrino would require a light-year of lead to have better than even odds of hitting anything. The Earth is not an obstacle. The Earth is a mild suggestion.
